patient_utterances: [Clinician asked: "good morning ms. reyes !"] good morning .
[Clinician asked: "how are you doing ma'am ?"] i'm doing well doctor , how are you ?
[Clinician asked: "i am fine thank you . so you've been having some problems with your right hip ?"] yeah .
[Clinician asked: "okay , and where are you hurting ? can you show me ?"] right in the groin area .
[Clinician asked: "okay , and this has been going on since february 2020 ?"] yeah .
[Clinician asked: "okay . and is it worse with movement ?"] well when it catches and i almost fall , yeah .
[Clinician asked: "okay . so it kinda grabs you ?"] yeah .
[Clinician asked: "okay , and this all started when you were walking ?"] well , walking around the infusion room .
[Clinician asked: "okay ."] so it started if i took a step back or , you know , stuff like that . now it happens anywhere .
[Clinician asked: "okay , so now it hurts whenever you move ?"] it hurts when i pivot .
[Clinician asked: "okay . so if you pivot then it hurts , got it . um ..."] anything can sometimes do it . sometimes it wo n't though , and sometimes it'll do it several times in a row .
[Clinician asked: "several times in a row , okay ."] and sometimes i fall .
[Clinician asked: "okay . and you rate the pain to range from two through seven out of 10 ?"] yeah , that's correct .
[Clinician asked: "okay . and are you experiencing fever or chills ?"] no .
[Clinician asked: "okay . and any tingling or numbness ?"] no .
[Clinician asked: "and have you had any problems with your bowel or bladder ?"] no .
[Clinician asked: "okay . and if you stay still , do you feel better ?"] yes , but i do n't want to stay still .

[Clinician asked: "i understand , no problem . and for past medical history , do you have anything going on ?"] i've had a lot of surgeries . i've had pcl , i had infertility , a gall bladder removed , but that's it .

[Clinician asked: "okay . and for family history , it looks like there's high blood pressure , diabetes , thyroid disease , heart disease , kidney disease and gastric ulcers . for your current medications , it does n't look like you're taking anything at this time . and you're allergic to percocet , vicodin and reguli"] yes , i've lost 110 pounds .
[Clinician asked: "that is awesome . and how did you do that ?"] with weight watchers .
[Clinician asked: "that's great ."] mm-hmm .
[Clinician asked: "and how many months have you been participating in weight watchers ?"] i started in 2018 , and i've been at my current weight for a little over a year .
[Clinician asked: "that is awesome ."] yeah , thank you .
[Clinician asked: "yeah , very good , and congratulations . and so , for social history , it looks like you work at an infusion center ?"] yes , over at .
[Clinician asked: "okay . and you live with your roommate , no history of tobacco and you limit alcohol intake to less than five drinks per month ."] that's correct .
[Clinician asked: "all right . well let's go ahead and take a look at your hip ."] okay .

[Clinician asked: "please use my general physical exam template . physical exam . ms. reyes is a pleasant 56-year-old woman who is five feet , six inches in height , weighing 169 pounds . blood pressure is 115 over 75 . pulse rate is 67 . ankles , no ankle edema is noted , no calf tenderness . okay , ms. reyes , can y"] okay .
[Clinician asked: "and can you walk on your tippy toes ? good , okay . and can you walk on your heels ? kind of a heel walk and toe walk are intact . um , go ahead and turn around please ."] okay .
[Clinician asked: "examination of the cervical spine , any pain here now ?"] no .

[Clinician asked: "okay , no tenderness . look at your right and your left and then over to the right , then go ahead and look up , then look down , and look straight ahead . range of motion is full in the neck without pain . spurling's test is negative . exam of the low back . any pain here ?"] no .
[Clinician asked: "okay . skin is intact , no midline tenderness to palpitation . go ahead and lean back . and lean to your right , to your left . does that hurt at all ?"] no .
[Clinician asked: "okay , great . and go ahead and bend forward and then come back up . and that does n't bother you ?"] no . i did or do have several bulging discs .
[Clinician asked: "okay . but you're not hurting right now ?"] no , the weight loss has really decreased all the pain .
[Clinician asked: "okay . range of motion is decreased in exertion . lateral flection without pain . any pain when i push ?"] no .
[Clinician asked: "okay . you can go ahead and , um , sit down please . no pain ?"] no .
[Clinician asked: "okay . sacroiliac signs are negative . examination of the hips . trochanteric is non tender . go ahead and lift your knee up , does that bother you ?"] um , just a little bit .
[Clinician asked: "okay , little bit . and then back one , probably bothers you ?"] right there , like there , yeah .
[Clinician asked: "okay . how about this way ? not too bad ?"] no .

[Clinician asked: "okay . range of motion is decreased in right hip with pain in the groin and internal and external rotation . okay , go ahead and keep it up , do n't let me push it down . does that hurt ?"] right there .
[Clinician asked: "okay . resisted right hip flection causes pain in the right groin region . no tenderness is noted . do you feel me touching you all the way down ?"] yeah .
[Clinician asked: "okay . motor control is normal in the lower extremities . go ahead and lift your knee up ."] okay .
[Clinician asked: "okay , lift it up . any pain ?"] no .
[Clinician asked: "okay . and this one ?"] yeah .

[Clinician asked: "and squeeze your knees together , push it out and kick your leg out straight . now go ahead and bring it back and kick it out straight again . and go ahead and lean back , keep it loose . okay , all set . you can go ahead and sit up now ."] okay , thank you .
[Clinician asked: "you're welcome . so what i think we're dealing with is right hip degenerative joint disease ."] okay .

[Clinician asked: "and we do have some options . so first is to start some low impact exercises . i can provide you with a hand out with what exercises you can do . you should take nsaids as needed to help with the pain and discomfort , as well as use of a cane to help offload the right side . a cane will help support"] hmm , i do n't love that idea but i'll give it a try .
[Clinician asked: "okay , that would be great . and we can also try a cortisone injection into the right hip joint to see if that offers any relief ."] i would like to definitely get the injection .

[Clinician asked: "okay . we can take care of that today while you're here and then schedule a follow up appointment in three months to see how you're doing , and then receive another injection if needed ."] okay , that sounds good .
[Clinician asked: "okay . and here are the risks associated with getting the inje- injection . um , please just take a moment to review it and consent to the shot ."] i'm good .
[Clinician asked: "great . we'll get that set up for ya . all right , well i hope things , um , feel better , and we will see you back here in three months ."] see you . have a nice day .
gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Right hip pain.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Elizabeth Reyes is a 56-year-old female who presents for the evaluation of pain in her right hip and groin region. Her symptoms started on 02/2020 and has progressively worsened. She is employed as an RN and notes that her symptoms began while she was working and walking around the infusion room at Johnson. Initially the pain would only happen when she took a step back, but the pain has progressed and now occurs with any type of movement involving her right hip and groin. She describes that her hip will sometimes “catch” when she moves, causing her to fall on occasion. The pain improves when she is still and stops moving. She rates her pain as 2-7/10.

Starting in 2018, the patient intentionally lost 110 pounds through Weight Watchers. She has maintained the weight loss and her weight has been stabile for the past 1 year. She has a history of several bulging discs, but the pain has significantly reduced due to her weight loss. Today, she reports no back pain.

She denies fever, chills, new onset of bowel or bladder dysfunction, tingling or numbness.

Opioid Assessment

Opioid Use: No.

Pain Assessment

Pain is frequent.
Quality of Pain: Right hip catches.
Intensity of Pain Using VAS 0-10 Scale (0 = No pain, 10 = Worst imaginable pain)
• Current Pain Intensity: 2/10.
• Average Pain Intensity Over the Past Week: 4/10.
• Pain at Best: 2/10.
• Pain at Worst: 7/10.
Relieving Factors: Lying down, sitting.
Aggravating Factors: Walking, pivoting, turning, any movement engaging right hip.

PAST HISTORY

Medical
PCOS.

Surgical
Cholecystectomy.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Works at the infusion center at Johnson. Lives with a roommate. Denies tobacco use. Limits alcohol intake to less than 5 drinks per month.

FAMILY HISTORY

Hypertension.
Diabetes.
Thyroid disease.
Kidney disease.
Gastric ulcers.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS

NSAID PRN by mouth.

ALLERGIES

Percocet.
Vicodin.
Reglan.

VITALS

Blood pressure: 115/75
Pulse: 67.
Height: 5’6.
Weight: 169 lb.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Constitutional
Pleasant.

Integumentary
Skin is in tact.

Musculoskeletal
Extremities: No bilateral ankle edema or calf tenderness.
Examination of gait: Heel-walk and toe-walk are intact.
Cervical spine exam: No tenderness is elicited. Range of motion is full in all planes without pain. Spurling's test is negative.
Lumbar spine exam: Range of motion is decreased in extension and lateral flexion without pain. No tenderness is elicited in the midline.
Sacroiliac joint exam: Bilateral sacroiliac joints are nontender to palpation.
Bilateral hip exam: Range of motion is decreased in the right hip with pain in the groin on internal and external rotation. Resisted right hip flexion causes pain in the right groin region. Bilateral trochanteric regions are nontender to palpation.

Neurological
Motor bulk and tone are normal in both lower extremities. Motor strength testing reveals no focal motor weakness in the lower extremities. Deep tendon reflexes are 1+ throughout. No focal sensory deficit is noted.

RESULTS

MR arthrogram of the right hip completed on 06/03/2021 reveals evidence of high-grade chondromalacia involving the anterosuperior right acetabulum with subchondral marrow edema and cyst formation.

ASSESSMENT

• Right hip degenerative joint disease.

PLAN

I discussed the clinical and radiological findings with the patient. Treatment options discussed are low impact exercises, use of analgesics as needed, and use of a cane to offload the right hip. She would like to proceed with a cortisone injection into her right hip joint. We will administer the injection today. I reviewed the procedure in detail, including the risks of the injection related to the use of steroid in the COVID setting. She understands the risks and would like to proceed with the injection.

INSTRUCTIONS

Schedule a follow up appointment in 3 months to assess her pain. If needed, a second injection may be administered.

naive.response_text:
I understand how frustrating and limiting this hip pain must be for you, especially since it's been going on for so long. It’s impressive that you’ve made such significant progress with your weight loss through Weight Watchers, and I’m glad it’s helped with other pain issues. However, I want to make sure we address this hip pain so you can continue to enjoy your life without it holding you back.

Based on what we discussed, it seems like the right hip is experiencing some degenerative changes, which can cause discomfort and instability, especially when pivoting. The treatment plan we’ve outlined includes exercises, medication, and a cortisone injection, all of which are designed to help reduce your pain and improve your mobility. I completely understand if you’re hesitant about using a cane—it’s not something anyone looks forward to—but it’s a simple tool that can really help protect your hip and prevent falls. 

The cortisone injection is a common and effective treatment for hip issues, and I’m happy to do it today so you can start feeling better sooner. We’ll schedule a follow-up in three months to see how things are progressing and decide if further treatment is needed. In the meantime, let’s work together to get you moving comfortably again. I’m confident that with these steps, we can help you feel more like yourself. I’m here for you, and we’ll take it one step at a time.

strong_prompt.response_text:
Thank you for sharing your concerns with me. It makes sense that you're worried about your hip pain, especially since it's been affecting you for over two years and has led to falls. I can understand how frustrating it must be, given how much your weight loss has helped with other pain issues but not this hip pain. Your efforts in managing your health are commendable, and I want to work together to find relief for you.

Based on your history and the physical exam, it seems like the right hip might be experiencing degenerative joint disease. This is a common issue, especially with the years of activity and previous surgeries you've had. To address this, we can start with some low-impact exercises to strengthen the area and improve mobility. I'll provide you with a handout for guidance. Additionally, using NSAIDs as needed can help manage the pain, and a cane might assist in offloading the affected side, which could reduce strain and prevent falls.

We also discussed the option of a cortisone injection, which can provide targeted relief. Since you're interested in this, we can proceed with it today. It's important to note that while effective, injections are typically used as part of a broader management plan. We'll schedule a follow-up in three months to assess your progress and decide if further treatment is needed.

Before you leave, could you summarize the plan in your own words? This will help ensure you understand the next steps. I'm here to answer any questions you might have and will follow up with you in three months to check on your progress. Let me know if you need anything else in the meantime.

framework_only.response_text:
Thank you for sharing your concerns and experiences with me today.

I want to make sure I explained this clearly — can you tell me, in your own words, what we're going to do next and why?

Let's plan to see each other again in 3 months. In the meantime, if you experience any new symptoms or if the pain worsens, please call us right away.

retrieval_only.response_text:
Thank you for sharing your concerns about your right hip. I understand that this has been an issue since February 2020 and it sounds like it's been affecting your daily activities, especially when walking or pivoting.

Hip issues can be complex, and the right approach often involves a team of healthcare professionals working together to address your concerns. This might include physical therapy, imaging studies, or other evaluations to better understand what's going on.

We'll arrange for some imaging to get a better idea of what's going on with your hip.

A referral to a physical therapist has been made to help manage your symptoms and improve mobility.

We'll also schedule a follow-up appointment in a few weeks to review the results and plan the next steps together.

full.response_text:
I understand you're having trouble with your right hip, especially when walking around the infusion room or pivoting. This seems to have been going on since February 2020, and I want to make sure we address it properly.

Based on the findings, we can perform some tests to check for issues like hip motion problems or pain during specific movements. These tests can help us determine if there's something like osteoarthritis or another condition that needs attention.

We'll start with some basic tests to check your hip motion and see if there's any pain during those movements.

Depending on the results, we might recommend further imaging or refer you to a specialist if needed.

We'll schedule a follow-up appointment in a few weeks to review the results and plan the next steps together.
